name: DGX System Architecture
description: Detailed architecture of NVIDIA DGX systems including GPU topology, memory, and interconnects
key_facts: DGX H100 contains 8 H100 SXM GPUs with 80 GB HBM3 each, totaling 640 GB of GPU memory
All 8 GPUs are connected via four NVSwitch chips enabling 900 GB/s per GPU bidirectional bandwidth
DGX H100 includes dual x86 CPUs, 2 TB system memory, and high-speed NVMe storage
The baseboard management controller (BMC) provides out-of-band system management and monitoring
DGX OS is a customized Ubuntu Linux distribution optimized for AI workloads with pre-installed drivers and tools
common_misconceptions: DGX systems use consumer-grade GPUs (they use data center SXM form factor GPUs with higher bandwidth and NVLink)
Upgrading individual GPUs in a DGX system is straightforward (GPUs are tightly integrated with NVSwitch and thermal design)

name: DGX SuperPOD Design
description: Architecture and deployment of DGX SuperPOD clusters for large-scale AI training
key_facts: DGX SuperPOD is a scalable AI infrastructure unit composed of multiple DGX nodes connected via InfiniBand fabric
A DGX H100 SuperPOD reference design starts with 32 DGX H100 nodes (256 GPUs) and scales further
InfiniBand NDR (400 Gb/s) switches provide the inter-node network fabric in SuperPOD deployments
NVIDIA Base Command Manager handles provisioning, scheduling, and management of SuperPOD resources
Shared parallel file systems like NVIDIA GPUDirect Storage and Lustre provide high-throughput data access
common_misconceptions: SuperPOD is a single monolithic system (it is a scalable cluster architecture built from individual DGX nodes)
Any network fabric works for SuperPOD (InfiniBand with specific topology requirements is essential for training performance)

name: GPU Cluster Topology Design
description: Network topology patterns and GPU placement strategies for AI training clusters
key_facts: Fat-tree topology provides full bisection bandwidth and is the standard for InfiniBand AI clusters
Rail-optimized topology reduces switch cost by connecting each GPU rail to its own network rail
GPU affinity ensures that communicating GPUs are placed on nodes with optimal network proximity
Non-blocking switch fabrics ensure that any pair of ports can communicate at full bandwidth simultaneously
Multi-tier switch architectures use leaf, spine, and core switches to scale cluster size
common_misconceptions: Any switch topology works for AI training (suboptimal topologies create network bottlenecks that severely degrade training performance)
More switches always means better bandwidth (oversubscription ratios determine actual available bandwidth)

name: Power and Cooling for GPU Clusters
description: Power delivery and thermal management strategies for high-density GPU infrastructure
key_facts: A single DGX H100 system consumes up to 10.2 kW of power at peak load
Direct liquid cooling is recommended for high-density GPU deployments exceeding 40 kW per rack
Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) and backup generators protect against power failures during long training runs
Hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment improves cooling efficiency in air-cooled GPU data centers
Power distribution units (PDUs) must be rated for the high amperage demands of GPU clusters
common_misconceptions: Standard data center cooling handles GPU racks (GPU racks generate 2-5x more heat than traditional compute racks)
Power efficiency improves linearly with more GPUs (power overhead from cooling and networking increases non-linearly)

name: InfiniBand Network Fabric
description: InfiniBand architecture, configuration, and management for AI training clusters
key_facts: InfiniBand NDR provides 400 Gb/s per port with sub-microsecond latency
Subnet Manager (SM) configures routing tables and manages the InfiniBand fabric
NVIDIA Quantum-2 switches provide up to 64 ports of NDR InfiniBand connectivity
Adaptive routing dynamically selects paths to avoid congestion in the InfiniBand fabric
SHARP (Scalable Hierarchical Aggregation and Reduction Protocol) offloads collective operations to network switches
common_misconceptions: InfiniBand is just faster Ethernet (InfiniBand has fundamentally different architecture with lossless fabric and RDMA built-in)
A single subnet manager is always sufficient (large fabrics benefit from standby SMs for high availability)

name: GPUDirect Technologies
description: NVIDIA GPUDirect RDMA, Storage, and peer-to-peer technologies for efficient data movement
key_facts: GPUDirect RDMA enables network adapters to directly read/write GPU memory without CPU involvement
GPUDirect Storage provides a direct data path between NVMe storage and GPU memory, bypassing system memory
GPUDirect Peer-to-Peer allows GPUs in the same system to directly access each other's memory over NVLink or PCIe
GPUDirect technologies reduce latency and CPU overhead for data transfers involving GPUs
NCCL uses GPUDirect RDMA for efficient inter-node collective communication during distributed training
common_misconceptions: GPUDirect only helps with training (it also significantly improves inference data loading and preprocessing performance)
GPUDirect RDMA works with any network adapter (it requires NVIDIA ConnectX or compatible InfiniBand/Ethernet adapters)

name: Parallel File Systems for AI
description: High-performance parallel file systems and storage solutions for AI training data
key_facts: Lustre and GPFS/Spectrum Scale are widely used parallel file systems for AI training data
Parallel file systems stripe data across multiple storage servers for aggregate throughput
NVMe-oF (NVMe over Fabrics) extends NVMe performance across the network for shared storage access
Data pipeline performance depends on achieving sufficient read throughput to keep GPUs saturated
Caching layers (local NVMe, in-memory) reduce repeated reads of frequently accessed training data
common_misconceptions: Network-attached storage (NAS) is sufficient for AI training (NAS typically lacks the throughput needed for multi-GPU data loading)
Storage performance only matters for data loading (checkpointing during training also requires high write throughput)

name: AI Data Pipeline Design
description: Designing efficient data pipelines for feeding training data to GPU clusters
key_facts: NVIDIA DALI (Data Loading Library) provides GPU-accelerated data preprocessing and augmentation pipelines
Prefetching overlaps data loading with GPU computation to hide I/O latency
Data format choices (TFRecord, WebDataset, Parquet) impact both I/O efficiency and preprocessing overhead
Multi-worker data loading parallelizes data read, decode, and transform operations across CPU cores
Object storage (S3-compatible) is commonly used for large-scale training datasets with streaming access
common_misconceptions: Data pipeline performance is limited by storage speed alone (CPU preprocessing and data format overhead are often the bottleneck)
GPU-accelerated data loading is only for image workloads (DALI supports tabular, audio, and video data as well)

name: AI Infrastructure Benchmarking
description: Tools and methodologies for benchmarking AI infrastructure performance
key_facts: MLPerf is the industry-standard benchmark suite for measuring AI training and inference performance
NVIDIA NCCL tests measure inter-GPU and inter-node communication bandwidth and latency
GPU memory bandwidth can be benchmarked using NVIDIA's bandwidthTest and STREAM benchmarks
Training throughput is measured in samples per second or tokens per second for language models
Inference benchmarks measure latency (P50, P95, P99) and throughput under varying concurrency levels
common_misconceptions: Peak theoretical performance reflects real-world throughput (actual performance is typically 40-70% of theoretical peak)
A single benchmark number captures system performance (different workloads stress different components)

name: Performance Profiling and Tuning
description: Tools and techniques for profiling and optimizing AI workload performance on GPU infrastructure
key_facts: NVIDIA Nsight Systems provides system-wide profiling of CPU, GPU, and network activity with timeline visualization
Nsight Compute provides detailed kernel-level GPU profiling including occupancy and memory throughput analysis
Common bottlenecks include CPU-GPU data transfer, memory bandwidth, kernel launch overhead, and synchronization
Mixed-precision training uses FP16 or BF16 for most operations with FP32 for critical accumulations
CUDA graph capture reduces kernel launch overhead by recording and replaying sequences of GPU operations
common_misconceptions: High GPU utilization means the workload is well optimized (the GPU may be busy with inefficient kernels)
Profiling is only needed during development (production workloads benefit from periodic profiling to catch regressions)
